    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Hi Anna,</p>
<p>“Toned” and “sculpted” are buzz words used in fitness marketing toward women, and you won’t find them used in my guides. However, in general, they mean to get leaner and more muscled, because that’s how you achieve that look. If you are particularly lean/skinny already, then simply gaining weight and muscle may give you the look you’re after. (More muscle at a similar fat level looks more “toned.”)</p>
<p>Bear in mind that there are two things we can control: muscle size and how much fat we carry.<br>
We cannot control where we hold fat, we can only control whether we carry more or less of it overall.<br>
We can obviously control which muscles we train, but generally, it’s best to train the full body.</p>
<p>Read this. It will help you set a direction: <a href="https://rippedbody.com/cut-or-bulk/" rel="ugc">Should I Bulk vs Cut (or Recomp)? The Definitive Guide</a></p>

</div>

    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Andy- I am a 74 inch tall, 196lb male. Lifting weights ~5 days a week for ~45 minutes a session. I am averaging 13k steps a day this month (most days 15-17k steps, others 8k-ish). At first I lost 30 lbs in 46 days. However the last 2 weeks I have lost only 1 pound a week. Still making strength gains in my lifts…not getting weaker yet. Due to the recently slowed weight loss I reduced my calorie intake down to 1500 calories a day. Not an exaggeration- I have 8 scoops of protein powder and 10 figs per day, nothing else. I have kept all activity levels the exact same and yet the scale still hardly budges. Strangely- I dont even feel hungry. Do you have any idea how this can be possible?</p>

</div>

    <div id="comment-92517" class="wpd-comment-right">
    <div class="wpd-comment-header">

    Andy Morgan
</div>
    <div class="wpd-comment-date" title="April 21, 2026 09:28">
    <i class="far fa-clock" aria-hidden="true"></i>
    April 21, 2026 09:28
</div>
    
    
    <div class="wpd-space"></div>
    <div class="wpd-comment-link wpd-hidden">
    <span wpd-tooltip="Comment Link" wpd-tooltip-position="left"><i class="fas fa-link" aria-hidden="true" data-wpd-clipboard="https://rippedbody.com/macro-calculator/#comment-92517"></i></span>

    <div class="wpd-reply-to">
    <i class="far fa-comments"></i>
    Reply to 
    <a href="#comment-92516">
        Brian
    </a>
</div>
    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Hi Brian, thank you for the question. Happy to explain what is going on.</p>
<p>46 days is ~7 weeks. Of the 30 lbs of weight loss, ~14 lbs of that is likely fat (2 lbs per week), and the rest water, gut content, and glycogen (WGG). </p>
<p>16 lbs of WGG loss is on the higher end of what we see, but it’s not unusual considering the type of diet you’re on will leave such a low residual gut content. (This is the type of trick that we might use for a <a href="https://rippedbody.com/making-weight/" rel="ugc">weight-class competitor — think fighter or powerlifter — who needs to drop weight for their weigh-in, from 7–10 days out</a>.)</p>
<p>If you weren’t aware of that (most aren’t), then the trend of what you’ve seen until now will have been very exciting. Anyway, assuming consistency in what you’ve been eating*, what you’ll have seen is rapid initial weight loss in the first 2–3 weeks, settling into a more steady 2 lbs per week. And if I have it right, then your ~2 lbs per week of weight loss has become 1 lb per week of weight loss, and you’re now wondering <em>why</em>, <em>if something is wrong</em>, and <em>what to do about it</em>. (*If you haven’t been consistent with your eating, then this trend will look considerably more messy, but the logic of what I am explaining still holds.)</p>
<p>The reason that weight loss has suddenly slowed is because of a change in WGG; most likely, you’re holding onto water. (That can happen due to stress, sleep issues, things mostly out of our control.) We can be certain of this, because energy needs drop gradually over the course of a diet, but not suddenly. So if diet adherence has been high and activity levels unchanged, the this is the only explanation.</p>
<p>Nothing is wrong. You didn’t need to change anything, so I would put the calories back up — just wait it out.</p>
<p>Does that make sense?</p>
<p>I have some further thoughts on what you’re doing here, if you’re open to them.</p>

</div>

    <div class="wpd-comment-date" title="April 22, 2026 10:49">

    <div class="wpd-space"></div>
    <div class="wpd-comment-link wpd-hidden">
    <span wpd-tooltip="Comment Link" wpd-tooltip-position="left"><i class="fas fa-link" aria-hidden="true" data-wpd-clipboard="https://rippedbody.com/macro-calculator/#comment-92523"></i></span>

    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Sure thing, Brian.</p>
<p>You are losing weight, and that’s great. But you’re doing it in a way that isn’t sustainable, and this will set you up for a rebound.</p>
<p>Why?</p>
<p>Well, none of your success so far comes down to addressing the things that caused you to be overweight in the first place: your environment and resulting habits. Once you finish dieting, the gravity of things will pull you back.</p>
<p>Further, you’re unlikely to quite get to where you wanted. The honeymoon phase is about to be over. The hunger is going to start ramping up, along with fatigue, and brain fog. Your performance in the gym is going to suffer, and you may start losing muscle. This is not to mention the potential health issues resulting from a nutritionally deficient diet.</p>
<p>I understand the desire to get the weight loss phase over with as fast as possible, but ultimately, this “fast” approach is more likely the longer journey.</p>
<p>If you were to ask my advice from here:<br>
1. Raise calorie intake to what it was before you cut it to 1500 recently, and add another 500 kcal. Assuming my previous numbers were roughly correct, this will net you ~1 lb per week of fat loss.<br>
2. <a href="https://rippedbody.com/how-to-count-macros/" rel="ugc">Learn to build some simple meals</a> that you enjoy and can therefore consistently eat without being bored, that hit your calorie budget that meet your protein targets.<br>
3. Accept that there will be a rise in scale weight because of this, but know that it is from WGG, not fat.<br>
4. <a href="https://rippedbody.com/diet-progress-tracking/" rel="ugc">Start tracking progress</a> as I recommend.</p>
<p>Take the Kickstart Course (see the box at the end of the article) — it’s free and it’ll teach you everything you need.</p>

</div>

    <p>Understood Andy, and thank you again for your wisdom here sir!!     <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Hi Brian,</p>
<p><em>would that still count as “active”?</em><br>
Yes, this is the very definition of active I have written in the calculator.</p>
<p><em>Because if I eat the daily 2183 calories the calculator would have me losing 2% bodyweight weekly at, I am not losing any weight at all, and im not feeling hungry in the slightest. That’s how I ended up dropping the calories to 1500.</em><br>
Don’t recalculate! Calculations are estimations. You already have a baseline of weight data and consistent calorie intake. Adjust per your current calorie baseline and aim for ~1 lb per week of weight loss from here.</p>
<p><em>Should I still just stick with 2200 cals daily and eventually the results will come?</em><br>
See above, then see point 2.</p>
<p><em>Is there a limit to how much fat one can lose in a day/week etc. before the rest is just pure muscle loss? Or is the concern just that the extreme measures are unsustainable and likely will gain the fat back when one returns to their former habits. </em><br>
Yes. The leaner you get and the faster you take things, the greater the risk of muscle loss. On the extreme ends, an obese person might get away with 2% of their body weight per week; a bodybuilding competitor weeks away from their show might aim for less than 0.5% of their body weight per week and still struggle to not lose a little muscle. If you click the “?” icon in the rate of weight loss selector you’ll see I have covered this there. You’ll see the recommended rates of weight loss here: <a href="https://rippedbody.com/calories/" rel="ugc">How To Set Your Calorie Intake For Fat Loss And Muscle Growth</a></p>
<p>Take the course and read the articles on the site. It will explain everything.</p>

</div>

    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Hi Dan, thanks for the question.</p>
<p>If you had your calorie intake tracked and daily weight-in data, it would be a case of some simple math.     <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Hi Ket, thank you for the question.</p>
<p>Yes, it’s a good idea to keep your fat intake to that lower bound. (I haven’t programmed the calculator to catch that.) You can do that by adjusting the fat slider in the results section. You’ll see that it keeps protein the same, and reduced carbs. As you’ll note in the book, there is a caveat to this: <em>“40–60 g/day minimum. May drop lower briefly in advanced bodybuilding contest prep phases.”</em></p>
<p>If 20% of your calorie intake is 28 g of fat, this means your target daily calorie intake is 1260. That’s very low. I didn’t understand your last sentence, but this is the kind of number that only happens in three circumstances:<br>
1. For small/short woman at the tail end of a long cut into a physique/bodybuilding show — a necessary evil to get further fat loss.<br>
2. The rate of weight loss has been set too aggressively.<br>
3. Activity levels are estimated too low, or are too low. </p>
<p>So, unless the first is true, consider adjusting for the others.</p>

</div>

    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>I have a bit of a question: Is cardio considered strength training? My workout schedule includes cardio and gym sessions on Wednesdays, just cardio on Thursdays, and cardio and gym sessions again on Fridays, with weekends dedicated only to the gym. I do spin classes for cardio, which really works my legs. Should I categorise that as just cardio and consider it a rest day or a training day when tracking my macros?     <span wpd-tooltip="Comment Link" wpd-tooltip-position="left"><i class="fas fa-link" aria-hidden="true" data-wpd-clipboard="https://rippedbody.com/macro-calculator/#comment-92443"></i></span>

    <i class="far fa-comments"></i>
    Reply to 
    <a href="#comment-92441">
        R Hussain
    </a>
</div>
    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Hi R., thank you for the question.</p>
<p>The spin class works your legs. This is a training stimulus. However, it is not a controlled stimulus that you’re managing carefully session to session to progressively overload, it’s a bit random, dependent on your energy levels and the instructor. For the purposes of the training vs. rest day distinction, consider only your resistance training to be a training day.</p>
<p>If the spin class is leaving you sore, this is either because you’re new to it and not used to it, or, you’re failing to recover (overdoing it). For most people it’s the former, and you just need a few sessions for the soreness to fade. Lighten the loads for your legs training as necessary while you get through it.<br>
If the soreness doesn’t fade, you’re overdoing it. Consider reducing the spin session frequency, duration, or intensity, and/or upping your calorie (particularly carb) intake.</p>

</div>

    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Hi Andy, I have been a fan and disciple for years now. I have probably asked you this question before but always good to refresh- lets say I eat much lower calories than suggested for the most aggressive fat loss, and I do TONS more steps (20k+ per day), so therefore I achieve what is recommended to take 5 months in 3 months…. is there any downside to this? To my understanding in the Muscle and Strength Pyramid, there isnt really much of a difference in muscle loss during a fat-loss period whether you lose it super quickly or super slowly. What are your thoughts?</p>
<p>And in this example, lets say I am still keeping protein at 1g/lb desired bodyweight, and carbs relatively high (low fat). For me personally, if I eat 2000 calories a day with a lot of cardio, I will easily lose 3lbs a week, often more, and 2000 per day is far from extreme starvation</p>

    </a>

    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Okay Andy I have gone back and studied Muscle and Strength Pyramid. It looks like some 2022 updates/studies offer a new perspective that I may have mistaken when I first read it in 2019. </p>
<p>To my understanding now- the leaner you get, the more you are at risk of losing muscle as you continue losing weight. So in the beginning of a fat loss phase you could be more aggressive, but as you start getting to roughly 13-15% bodyfat you shouldn’t go for a huge deficit, as you’ll be more at risk of losing muscle from this point on. </p>
<p>Also the bigger the deficit & the “harder” you are starving, your gym performance and recovery will surely suffer, which will translate to losing more muscle aside from what you would already by losing purely from being in a state of loss. </p>
<p>Lastly, spending 3.33hrs a day doing steps to reach 20k steps a day is not only a massive commitment of your time (and mental energy/patience), but this can also slow your recovery and wear you down</p>
<p>Sound about right?</p>

</div>

    </a>

    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Hi Usman,<br>
If you have factored this activity into the multiplier, it’s not necessary to adjust.</p>
<p><strong>Broader sanity check:</strong> Some days you’ll be more or less active than others. This may be through planned activity (like what you have mentioned), or it can be through unplanned activity (your friend spontaneously invites you on a long walk). Typically, this will mean your planner deficit or surplus will be up a few hundred calories off what you had planned. — This is ok. What matters is what happens globally (over a whole week), not daily. So, I don’t recommend daily adjustments for different activity levels:</p>
<p>1. It makes consistency harder.<br>
2. Even if you can do it, it doesn’t lead to a better result.<br>
3. Micromanaging things is bad for the soul.<br>
4. These numbers are all estimations anyway.</p>

</div>

    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Hello Andy,<br>

    <div class="wpd-comment-date" title="November 29, 2025 09:07">
    <i class="far fa-clock" aria-hidden="true"></i>

    <div class="wpd-space"></div>
    <div class="wpd-comment-link wpd-hidden">
    <span wpd-tooltip="Comment Link" wpd-tooltip-position="left"><i class="fas fa-link" aria-hidden="true" data-wpd-clipboard="https://rippedbody.com/macro-calculator/#comment-92122"></i></span>

    <div class="wpd-reply-to">
    <i class="far fa-comments"></i>
    Reply to 
    <a href="#comment-92118">
        Don Gillespie
    </a>
</div>
    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Hi Don,</p>
<p>Ah, the activity multiplier is in the activity level settings. You mean you upped the rate of weight loss to 1% from the default of 0.75% per week. This will indeed increase the calorie deficit, making the diet tougher to adhere to.</p>
<p>There isn’t a number I can give you that is “too low,” as it’s all relative. But you’ve got to do what you can sustain. This is a marathon, not a sprint. If you already believe you’ll find this too tough, or if you are in doubt, start with a more modest rate of fat loss. (We tend to recommend clients around the 0.5% rate.) </p>
<p>If you are finding things too easy you can always adjust your intake later to be more aggressive. This is a better approach than the ubiquitous opposite that ends in a rebound.</p>
<p>Everyone wants faster results, but the fastest sustained results often come from setting the rate of weight loss slower rate, because most people don’t succeed otherwise.</p>
<p>Does that make sense?</p>

</div>
    <div class="wpd-comment-footer">
    
    <div class="wpd-reply-button">

    <div class="wpd-reply-to">
    <i class="far fa-comments"></i>
    Reply to 
    <a href="#comment-92126">
        Don Gillespie
    </a>
</div>
    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Given that you have a lot of weight to lose, yes.<br>
If you keep protein intake to the amounts recommended, that will be sufficient. Eating more will not mitigate this risk further.<br>
The faster you take things, the less less likely you are to gain any muscle.<br>
The leaner you get, the greater the risk to muscle loss, and the slower I recommend you take it. (See the recommended range in the notes.)</p>

</div>

    <a href="#comment-92108">
        James V.
    </a>
</div>
    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Hi James, thank you for the question.</p>
<p>Yes.<br>
1. It takes a 500 kcal deficit to lose 1 lb of fat per week (1100 kcal per 1 kg).<br>
2. The calculator defaults to 0.75% of body weight per week, which in your case is 0.94 kg per week.<br>
3. 0.94*1100 = 1031 kcal.</p>
<p>If you feel this may be too tough, feel free to nudge the “Cutting Rate” slider lower.</p>
<p>“When you do a CUT does this include weight training as well or just purely diet?”<br>
Weight training is factored into most of the Activity Level options.</p>

</div>

    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Hi Ta’mara, glad you have found the calculator useful. </p>
<p>What is it specifically about this calculator that gives you hope? That might help me understand.</p>
<p><a href="https://rippedbody.com/calories/" rel="ugc">Calorie balance</a> is the most important. You can target the specific <a href="https://rippedbody.com/macros/" rel="ugc">macronutrients</a> if you wish (and many clients do), but calorie balance and protein intake are the two most important aspects. That said, carbs play a role in fueling workouts and so you may get a better result by not restricting them heavily, which it sounds like you have been. 50 g of carbs per meal shouldn’t be hard to hit. That’s a relatively small portion of rice, pasta, or even just two bananas.</p>

</div>

    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Thank you for replying so quickly. I’ve been trying to figure out how to do macros for fat loss/toning but everything on the web is confusing or contradictory. Your calculator is a god send and gave me clear parameters to meal prep. I’m skinny fat and building muscle is hard but I’m doing it. I just am not ripped for lack of better terminology. I want more definition that is what I’m lacking. Before the calculator put it into perspective just how many carbs I needed to eat I was lucky to be hitting 1/4 of what is needed. I had no idea you needed to eat so much and primarily focused on high protein and healthy fats like Avocado. </p>
<p>It is still hard for me to eat enough food but I’m at 121 P, 144 C, 24 F today. I did sweet potatoes and banana for breakfast carbs, buckwheat and raspberries for lunch carbs, chic peas and apple for snack carbs, and brown rice and green beans for dinner carbs. Stuffed to say the least, but I am going to give this a committed try and see how my body responds,</p>
<p>Thank you</p>

</div>

    <img alt="David C." src="https://secure.gravatar.com/avatar/f6c3ff2b92c36749211de5daf82927e7010a16af2de317191bae4b5e089ce679?s=64&d=identicon&r=g" srcset="https://secure.gravatar.com/avatar/f6c3ff2b92c36749211de5daf82927e7010a16af2de317191bae4b5e089ce679?s=128&d=identicon&r=g 2x" class="avatar avatar-64 photo" height="64" width="64" loading="lazy" decoding="async">

    <div id="comment-91905" class="wpd-comment-right">
    <div class="wpd-comment-header">
    <div class="wpd-comment-author ">
    David C.
</div>
    <div class="wpd-comment-date" title="September 16, 2025 10:39">
    <i class="far fa-clock" aria-hidden="true"></i>
    September 16, 2025 10:39
</div>
    
    
    <div class="wpd-space"></div>
    <div class="wpd-comment-link wpd-hidden">
    <span wpd-tooltip="Comment Link" wpd-tooltip-position="left"><i class="fas fa-link" aria-hidden="true" data-wpd-clipboard="https://rippedbody.com/macro-calculator/#comment-91905"></i></span>
</div>
</div>
    
    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>I have one question: calculating maintenance TDEE.</p>
<p>I’m 133 lbs. For 16 days, I’ve tracked my bodyweight and calories, weighing all my food, except when I made estimates for the three meals I ate out. Using 3500 cal = 1lb, I’ve computed my maintenance at more than 3,000 cal/day.</p>
<p>This seems outrageously high! I’m 35, 5’7″, have a sedentary job, and I lift heavy 4 days/week. With that profile, all the TDEE calculators say my maintenance should be 700 calories lower.</p>
<p>Is 3,000 cal/day a realistic possibility? It’s true that all my life I’ve had a voracious appetite and eaten enormous amounts without gaining weight. I’m lucky. Still, I’m shocked the number overshadows the TDEE calculators by so much. I love nerding out with data, but 3,000 calories/day seems crazy, and I don’t know if hardgainers are really all that common. I’d appreciate hearing if I can trust this figure enough to make nutritional decisions, or if I’m way off and need to recalibrate.</p>
<p>Thanks!</p>

</div>
    <div class="wpd-comment-footer">
    
    <div class="wpd-reply-button">

    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Hi David, thanks for the question.</p>
<p>Let’s set aside the possibility that you’re overestimating what you eat.</p>
<p>Sure, it’s certainly possible. Calculators are estimations based on equations that are derived from group averages. There are outliers, and you already have a very good idea from experience that you are one.</p>
<p>The math is the math. Don’t second-guess it. <img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="💪" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f4aa.svg"></p>

</div>

    <span>Reply</span>

</div>
</div>
    
    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>What is your opinion on the lack of evidence supporting the benefits of more then 1.6g/Kg/day ? (I use the calculators and normally they recommend above this).<br>
Another question: regarding the articles saying the risks of high protein intake? I understand some of them are related to animal protein (and some cofounding factors such as fat may be involved) – but could that explain the risks?</p>
<p>In this article you will find the references to the articles: <a href="https://erictopol.substack.com/p/our-preoccupation-with-protein-intake" rel="nofollow ugc">https://erictopol.substack.com/p/our-preoccupation-with-protein-intake</a></p>
<p>Thank you</p>

</div>
